name: Roman wall of Lugo – circular via the Ponte Vella and the Porta de San Pedro
name_original: Muralla romana de Lugo – circular por el Ponte Vella y la Porta de San Pedro
region_intro: Waterfall hiking trails around the Mio are characterized by diverse landscapes, including the scenic Au Sable River and the expansive Huron National Forest. The region features a mix of groomed gravel paths, boardwalks through wetlands, and rolling hills. These natural settings provide varied woodland environments with cedar, oak, and pine trees, alongside numerous lakes and marshes. Best waterfall hiking trails around the Mio The most popular waterfall hiking route is Augacaída Waterfall and Castro de Marce – circular from Marce, a 3.1 miles (4.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 29 minutes to complete. This easy route features the Augacaída Waterfall and views of Castro de Marce. Another top favourite among local hikers is Pozas de Melon and Fervenza Touron - circular along the Cerves River, an easy 3.8 miles (6.1 km) path. This trail follows the Cerves River, offering views of Pozas de Melon and Fervenza Touron. Local hikers also love the Camino Natural del Río Barbantiño – circular from A Forxa, a 7.5 miles (12.1 km) trail leading through riverine landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 2 minutes. Hiking around the Mio is defined by river valleys, diverse forest ecosystems, and wetlands, offering a range of experiences from gentle strolls to more challenging paths. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy to difficult. The routes in Mio are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 350 reviews. More than 3000 hikers have used komoot to explore Mio's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many waterfall hiking trails are available around Mio?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;14&lt;/strong&gt; distinct waterfall hiking trails around Mio, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These routes traverse diverse landscapes, including the scenic Au Sable River and the expansive Huron National Forest.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for waterfall hikes in Mio?
A: The waterfall hikes around Mio cater to a range of abilities. You'll find &lt;strong&gt;6 easy routes&lt;/strong&gt;, &lt;strong&gt;7 moderate routes&lt;/strong&gt;, and &lt;strong&gt;1 more challenging route&lt;/strong&gt;. This variety ensures there's a suitable option whether you're looking for a gentle stroll or a more strenuous adventure.

Q: Are there any circular waterfall walks in the Mio area?
A: Yes, several waterfall hikes around Mio are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979353635/augacaida-waterfall-and-castro-de-marce-circular-from-marce" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall and Castro de Marce – circular from Marce&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.1-mile loop, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e976213063/pozas-de-melon-and-fervenza-touron-circular-along-the-cerves-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozas de Melon and Fervenza Touron - circular along the Cerves River&lt;/a&gt; offers a 3.8-mile circular path.

Q: What makes Mio a unique destination for waterfall hiking?
A: Mio's unique appeal for waterfall hiking stems from its location within the Huron National Forest and along the Au Sable River. Trails often wind through diverse woodlands of cedar, oak, and pine, feature boardwalks through wetlands, and offer picturesque views of the river. The region's varied terrain, from rolling hills to river valleys, provides a rich backdrop for exploring its waterfalls.

Q: Are the waterfall trails around Mio suitable for families with children?
A: Many of the waterfall trails around Mio are suitable for families, especially the &lt;strong&gt;6 easy routes&lt;/strong&gt; available. These often feature groomed paths or boardwalks, making them accessible for younger hikers. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979353635/augacaida-waterfall-and-castro-de-marce-circular-from-marce" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall and Castro de Marce – circular from Marce&lt;/a&gt; is a popular easy option that families might enjoy.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I expect to see along the waterfall hikes?
A: Beyond the waterfalls themselves, you can discover a variety of natural attractions. The region is characterized by the scenic Au Sable River, diverse forest ecosystems, and wetlands. You might encounter highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5016863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the expansive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6496544" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribeira Sacra River Valley&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5795615" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miño River&lt;/a&gt;, all contributing to the area's natural beauty.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the waterfall trails in Mio?
A: The waterfall trails in Mio are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of &lt;strong&gt;4.5 stars&lt;/strong&gt; from over 350 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the diverse landscapes, the serene river views, and the well-maintained paths that make exploring the region's natural beauty a rewarding experience.

Q: Is there parking available near the waterfall trailheads?
A: While specific parking details vary by trailhead, many trails are accessible. For instance, the Wolf Creek Trail, a significant non-motorized path in the region, is accessible from downtown Mio and has a second access point. The Mio Pond State Forest Campground also serves as a base for exploring and offers nearby walking trails.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks near the waterfall hiking routes?
A: Yes, the Mio area offers several interesting historical and cultural landmarks. You can explore sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/751631" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portomarín Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/137061" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of St John of Portomarín&lt;/a&gt;, or the grand &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/334304" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of Saint Mary of Lugo&lt;/a&gt;. The ancient city of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5151829" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lugo&lt;/a&gt; itself is a notable historical site.

Q: What is the best time of year to visit Mio for waterfall hiking?
A: While waterfalls can be enjoyed year-round, spring offers vibrant greenery and fuller falls due to snowmelt, while autumn provides stunning autumnal foliage alongside the cascades. The Huron National Forest's diverse tree species create a beautiful backdrop during these seasons. Winter hiking is also possible on some trails, offering a different, serene experience.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the waterfall hiking trails in Mio?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many trails within the Huron National Forest, but it's always best to check specific trail regulations or campground rules before heading out. Always ensure your dog is on a leash and that you clean up after them to preserve the natural environment for everyone.

Q: What should I wear for waterfall hiking in Mio, especially in spring?
A: In spring, weather can be variable. We recommend dressing in layers, including a waterproof or water-resistant outer shell, as trails near waterfalls can be damp or muddy. Sturdy, waterproof hiking boots with good grip are essential. Consider bringing insect repellent, especially for wetland areas, and always carry water and snacks.

Q: Are there any less-traveled or 'hidden gem' waterfall hikes in the area?
A: While specific 'hidden gems' are often discovered by local exploration, trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38256817" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall loop from Vilar&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14935581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Augacaída Waterfall – Castro de Marce Viewpoint loop from Torre de Marce&lt;/a&gt; might offer a more secluded experience compared to the most popular routes. Exploring the extensive network of trails within the Huron National Forest often leads to peaceful discoveries.
tips: The gate of San Pedro is one of the 10 old entrances to the walled city of Lugo. The evidence that would show that it already existed in Roman times under the name of Sancti Petri, when the town was known as Lucus Augusti, is different.

The church of San Froilán belonged to the old hospital of San Juan de Dios. It is located in Plaza El Ferrol, near the Puerta Falsa.
Its origin was a chapel linked to the disappeared hospital of San Bertomeu, founded in 1621, and which a hundred years later became administered by the brothers of San Juan de Dios.

Built more than 17 centuries ago following the guidelines of the elegant works of Vitruvius, the Wall of Lugo measures more than 2 km and has 10 gates. Walking along the top of the wall that in some sections reaches 7 m wide, and stopping at some of its 71 towers that are preserved (of the original 85) is to feel the power of Imperial Rome up close. And also, enjoy the best views.

This impressive 12th-century cathedral has undergone various modifications over the years. Although it mixes different architectural styles, it is mostly Romanesque. It is necessary to book a ticket to visit the interior. You can find more information about schedules and fees on this page: https://catedraldelugo.es/visita-cultural-3/.

This beautiful Roman bridge passes over the Miño river and connects the city of Lugo with the old road to Santiago. Although it was adapted for vehicles and pedestrians, in 2009 a reform was carried out in which all the asphalt was removed and it was covered with a cobbled walkway so that it resembled the time in which it was built.

waypoints.name: Cathedral of Saint Mary of Lugo
